author: @GEOsustainable
text: I wish this info had been available a few years back. I had an assignment to pick a wind turbine and I chose a horizontal axis. The professor gave me a B because it was small looking compared to everyone else's. I never got to show how efficient it was compared to theirs. Horizontal is the best use of available wind. Thanks Robert. My idea was the blades were vertical, but the hub was horizontal. This way the blades can have the length needed for max torque. I've seen stone mills from ancient times built like this.

author: @kjvisual7
text:
This is really helpful information.

 Question. I am imagining a wind system that utilizes a flywheel and some sort of momentum assist. The power that is generated would be stored in a battery (just enough to power an appliance and or TV). 

The wind would be the primary force to keep pressure on the turbine. A flywheel would maintain momentum and help counter the resistance from a motor or magnets. The assist would apply short minimal bursts of spinning force to maintain the flywheel momentum to help even out wind consistency. The assist could be powered by house power or solar (battery). 

What do you think about that kind of system?

author: @Kangsteri
text: You might be interested to try reversing "The Spinning Drone Paradox" for more officiant generator. The flywheel effect and angular momentum is forgotten and not counted too often :/

author: @pikotech1
text:
Brilliant video again Rob. Similar wind speeds up here where I am in Yorkshire.

It made me think, instead of 1 turbine, multiple with smaller blades works far better at that low wind speed.

For me, the sweet spot seems to be about 20cm blade (generates around 26 Watts). But if you were to create 100 of those (not a small task, but certainly not impossible if you have a decent garden or double garage) and put them on a pitched roof, you're seeing better than a typical 2.4kw solar install! It would also work day and night, and 2.6kw is after accounting for efficiency, which a typical 2.4kw solar install doesn't. 

Just a random thought. It made me think of the tiny windmills we all used to have with our buckets and spades at the beach. I wonder if lots of micro generation in those low wind speeds might be a good experiment (similar to your wind wall of course).

Multiple tiny turbines are perhaps not only the solution for home energy, they're also incredibly easy to build with parts lying around at home.

author: @Titanium369
text:
So many questions. Where is the wind registered? Heights of calculation? Valley /mound ?  Above sea levels?
Any restrictions from the landscape around you?

Trying to control these is a simple solution. Heights. The higher the wind turbine usually equates to high wind output/collection. (W /Limits)

author: @ruthcormack1765
text: I would say that you almost always will have to save the energy in a battery or two AND couple with solar panels to make it a worthwhile project on this scale. I did that back in 2002 on an allotment portakabin lighting project using tree saver bulbs with TWO car batteries, one on a 10 minute pushbutton retrigger while the other was loaded. I designed a high efficiency bespoke inverter to suit. A friend of mine made the dynamo-bike-wheel turbine & we stuck the SP & turbine on the portakabin roof.

author: @azlandpilotcar4450
text: Best engineering design process description!

author: @saiello2061
text:
I've had this argument with others building wind turbines who profess they have built the most efficient turbine ever! I always reply that there's no universally efficient turbine, the "best" turbine depends entirely on your local wind profile. Even average wind speeds for a locality might lead you astray, where wind speed(power)/frequency profiles would be better data to inform a design. You could have hurricane force winds on one day of the year and gentle breezes on the other 364 days. Taking an average here would be virtually useless 😋.

Incidentally, I've been looking at efforts by individuals on YouTube in creating cheap solar devices using thin reflective/mylar films as solar concentrators to heat water/oil, with surprising results. Commercial installations of this type exist but I think this is an area well worth investigating that anyone can cheaply mess around with. Maybe combine with some form of sand "battery"? I find it hard to believe that a solar concentrator is going to be a less efficient collector than the 20% of a PV panel... 🤔 Maybe Luke might like to have a crack at this tech... 😁

author: @AmandaBrecc
text: Question. If we are using a VAWT then why not incorporate into the base, a flywheel. Instead of taking everything from center or attaching the generator to the shaft of the generator, use the flywheel as your gear ratio. Maybe maybe not but with a flywheel, like alternators turned generators more than one can be used and the flywheel can be on ground level only put in motion by the wind higher up leaving the generator at ground level if any work or maintenence needs be done.

author: @smob0
text: I think we need to take a step back and think about how we do things. 200W isn't really a whole lot right now, but if you didn't have to worry so much about your major appliances, ie your fridge, heating, hot water, cooking, clothes washing etc, then 200W would go a long way towards powering led lights, and modern electronics.

replies.author: @scotttovey
replies.text:
So, your going to spend a few hours a week washing your clothes in a tub with a wash board?

Do that a time or two and you will realize very quickly why the washing machine was invented.

While you can keep things cool with alternatives to the fridge, use an alternative way to heat, use an alternative way to heat your water, and use an alternative to cook; the fact of the matter is; modern appliances are more efficient and more convenient than all those ancient alternatives.

You are going to use energy until you are dead, and the elitist environmentalist wackos prefer you to be dead rather than using energy.

When dealing with people like that, you are better off pulling the trigger first and asking questions later, because when  you do develop a way to produce more energy cheaper; they will have a fit that will give you no other choice but to pull the trigger. 

You cannot compromise or work with; delusional monomaniacal people.

replies.author: @Teknopottu
replies.text:
There are different types of fridges but 200W generated for couple of hours a day should be enough, given there are batteries to store that energy. 

Every watt generated is a win-win situation and while 200W isn't enough for all electrical needs, perhaps an another wind generator will also generate something. Maybe solar panels will do their thing. Maybe there is a small steam generator whirling at the backyard. 

Most of the appliances we use do not require power all the time so even a little generation stored is going to help.
